title: $q$-Exponential Random Graphs: higher-order networks from simple constraints
authors: David Dobáš, Diego Garlaschelli, Petr Jizba
abstract: Exponential Random Graphs (ERGs) are among the most widely used network models, derived as principled least-bias graph ensembles that maximize Shannon entropy under constraints on the expected values of given structural properties. However, it has been recently (re)discovered that, in the absence of additional information privileging Shannon entropy, the most agnostic inferential construction should maximize the broader class of Uffink entropies. The resulting entropy-maximizing distribution changes from the exponential (Boltzmann-Gibbs) to the so-called q-exponential one. Since maximizing Shannon entropy may produce an unjustified independence between degrees of freedom, here we investigate how the most popular ERGs with independent edges (namely, the Erdos-Renyi and configuration models) generalize to higher-order q-Exponential Random Graphs with dependent edges in the non-Shannon case, while keeping their defining constraints (number of links and degree sequence, respectively) unchanged. We find features, such as a phase transition between sparse and dense regimes, that are absent in the original ERGs but typical of higher-order networks, plus novel phenomena such as richer assortativity and clustering profiles, which allow for the coexistence of link sparsity and triadic closure. These results show that higher-order networks do not necessarily require higher-order constraints, as they naturally arise from simpler ones in a framework that is even more agnostic than Shannon's.
summary: This work develops $q$-Exponential Random Graph Models by replacing the standard Shannon entropy maximization with generalized $q$-entropies. This generalization allows the model to capture higher-order network correlations, such as tunable clustering and phase transitions, using only simple constraints. It shows that the non-Shannon parameter $q$ acts as a control knob for these complex structural features.
main_problem: The paper generalizes the Maximum Entropy Principle (MEP) for network ensemble construction from the standard Shannon entropy to a broader class of $q$-entropies (Uffink entropies). It seeks to model complex networks in a more agnostic way than the standard Boltzmann-Gibbs framework.
main_result: The resulting $q$-Exponential Random Graphs ($q$-ERGs) and $q$-Exponential Configuration Models ($q$-CMs) exhibit higher-order structural dependencies, such as tunable clustering and phase transitions, even when only simple constraints (like total link count or degree sequence) are imposed.
method: The methodology involves maximizing generalized entropy functionals (like Tsallis entropy) subject to expected structural constraints, leading to $q$-exponential probability distributions. This is analyzed through mathematical derivations, saddle-point approximations, and MCMC simulations.
model_or_system: The model is the random graph ensemble, generalizing the Erdős–Rényi and Configuration Models. The core mathematical structure is the $q$-exponential distribution, $P_q(G) \propto \exp_{2-q}(-\sum 	ilde{	heta}_i c_i(G))$, where $q$ controls the deviation from the standard Shannon case.
key_observables: Link density, degree-resolved clustering coefficient $\langle C angle(k)$, average nearest-neighbor degree $\langle k_{nn} angle(k)$, and the order parameter $S$ (fraction of nodes in the largest connected component).
important_parameters: The entropic parameter $q$ (or $r = 1-q$), which controls the degree of non-extensivity and the strength of higher-order correlations.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that maximizing generalized entropies provides a more agnostic description of physical systems than maximizing Shannon entropy. The analysis relies on consistent generalizations of the Maximum Entropy Principle.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how the non-Shannon parameter $r$ independently controls higher-order properties like clustering ($\langle C angle$) and nearest-neighbor degree ($\langle k_{nn} angle$) in the $q$-CM, while the $q$-ER model shows enhanced critical fluctuations near the percolation threshold.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the mathematical necessity of generalized entropies, then applying this framework to generalize the ER and CM models, deriving the $q$-exponential forms, and finally analyzing the resulting structural properties, phase transitions, and the independent control offered by the parameter $r$.
why_interesting: The framework provides a powerful, information-theoretic tool to model complex network structures that exhibit non-trivial correlations (like high clustering at low density) without needing to explicitly add higher-order constraints to the model Hamiltonian.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The effective free entropy density ϕr(c, θ) for r = 2.7 as a function of link density c for different values of θ. The global maxima of these curves determine the typical configu- ration.

FIG. 2: On the left, we show the behavior of the typical configuration link density cmax for r = 2 as a function of θ. On the right, for r = 2.7, we plot the local and global maxima of the effective free entropy density ϕr(c, θ) as a function of θ. In the range θ ∈[4.831, 5.846], there are two local maxima clow and chigh, where one is stable and one metastable. The phase transition happens at θcrit ≈5.154. For reference, we also show the Shannonian network density cShannon = (1 + eθ)−1.

FIG. 4: For different values of r, we numerically approximated boundaries of the region, where density reconstruction in the q-ER model is not possible. Such a region is determined by the lowest possible value of chigh and the highest possible value of clow in the metastable regime. We also show r value of the critical point, rcp = 2.2767.

FIG. 3: The phase diagram of the q-ER model. For each value of r, we show the spinodal points θsp−and θsp+ as well as the phase transition θcrit. The critical point (rcp, θcp) ≈ (2.2767, 4.3994) is shown as the black dot.

FIG. 5: For ensembles of 1000 graphs with 100 nodes, we compute the average link density ⟨c⟩and compare with the perturbative linear correction [Eq. (91)] and with the solutions of the saddle-point equation [Eq. (71), on the right].
